Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.

Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 345 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 5.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I've collected red and blue. Now I need to move the player to change the rows of these blocks, then retract. Let me move the player UP to a high row, then retract. This will return red to cols 18-21 at the new (higher) row, while blue stays at cols 24-27. Then I can collect blue separately.
But wait, I'm running very low on time (72 seconds remaining). Let me think about what the simplest solution might be.
Looking at the current state:
- Red and blue are collected (at end of path)
- Green is at rows 37-40, cols 18-21
If I move the player DOWN to below green, then retract, red and blue should return to cols 18-21 at the new (lower) row. The order would be: green (37-40), red/blue (lower).
But the target order is red, green, blue (top to bottom). So I need red at the top, green in the middle, blue at the bottom.
Actually, let me reconsider the problem. Maybe I should just try to get all three blocks in the correct vertical order: red (top), green (middle), blue (bottom).
Current state (before my recent actions):
- Red: rows 31-34, cols 18-21
- Blue: rows 31-34, cols 24-27
- Green: rows 37-40, cols 18-21
The target order is red, green, blue. Currently red and blue are at the same row, green is below. I need to move blue below green.
Let me try: move player DOWN to below green, retract. Red returns to cols 18-21 at the new row. Blue stays at cols 24-27. Then I can collect blue (now at cols 24-27) separately, move player, and retract to position blue below green.
But I'm running low on time. Let me just try moving DOWN and retracting to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move DOWN to position below green, then retract
action(['DOWN'] * 5)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
